title: Coherent room-temperature dipole synchronization in nanocavity sheets
authors: Rakesh Arul, Piper Fowler-Wright, Lille Borresen, Brendon W. Lovett, Jonathan Keeling, Jeremy J. Baumberg
abstract: Plasmonic nanocavities enable the synchronization of spatially distant emissive dipoles through strong near-field coupling in sub-nm gaps. We report formation of a room-temperature synchronized dipole state in locally-ordered plasmonic nanogap 2D arrays under non-resonant continuous-wave pumping. Unlike lasers, photonic Bose-Einstein condensates, or exciton-polariton condensates, this system exhibits spatial coherence across the dipoles, while rapid radiative and non-radiative emission suppresses temporal photon coherence. A change of behaviour is observed with increasing pumping, marked by the spatial spread of g(1) coherence, but without spectral narrowing or directional emission. This driven-dissipative system exhibits fast temporal coherence decay and complex spatial correlations, offering a new platform for studying synchronization at room temperature. Combining ultralow mode volumes, high Purcell enhancement, and scalable ambient operation, it opens pathways for novel photonic and quantum technologies.
summary: The research reports on achieving coherent dipole synchronization at room temperature using plasmonic nanocavity arrays. By combining experiments measuring spatial coherence with theoretical modeling of driven-dissipative dynamics, the authors demonstrate that phase locking occurs robustly despite high losses. This platform offers a novel route for studying quantum coherence in scalable, ambient photonic devices.
main_problem: The research aims to demonstrate and characterize coherent dipole synchronization at room temperature within plasmonic nanocavity sheets, distinguishing this phenomenon from standard phase transitions.
main_result: The system achieves robust spatial coherence and synchronization despite high dissipation and incoherent pumping, exhibiting a unique crossover behavior rather than a sharp phase transition. The emission profile shows expansion into a 'halo' region at high pump powers.
method: The study combines experimental measurements of spatial coherence ($g^{(1)}(R)$) using Michelson interferometry with theoretical modeling employing master equations and cumulant expansions.
model_or_system: The platform is locally-ordered plasmonic nanogap 2D arrays containing organic dye emitters (Methylene blue). The dynamics are modeled using a tight-binding Hamiltonian coupled to the cavity modes, governed by a master equation incorporating pumping and decay rates.
key_observables: Spatial coherence $g^{(1)}(R)$, photon-photon correlations ($N_{nm}$), local emitter coherence ($\langle Z_n angle$), and emission intensity maps showing 'halo' formation.
important_parameters: High light-matter coupling ($g > 100 	ext{ meV}$), high photon loss ($\kappa > 100 	ext{ meV}$), and the critical pump power ($P_{th}$) marking regime changes.
main_assumptions: The theoretical model relies on a second-order cumulant expansion, and the measurement of $g^{(1)}(R=0,	au=0)$ is noted to be an average over multiple nanogaps.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate setup details, intensity maps showing halo formation (Fig. 1), spatial coherence decay profiles using a Michelson interferometer (Fig. 2), nonlinear scaling of emission power vs. pump strength (Fig. 3), and theoretical correlation spreading ($N_{nnn}$, $C_{nn}$) (Fig. 4).
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the physical system, detailing the governing master equation and measurement techniques, presenting results on coherence measurements and nonlinear scaling, and finally discussing advanced diagnostics like vortex detection to characterize the non-equilibrium nature of the synchronization.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it addresses driven-dissipative quantum phenomena (synchronization) in a solid-state, ambient environment. The distinction between this mechanism and traditional BEC/laser transitions provides new insights into open quantum systems dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(d) Each emitter is incoherently pumped by a laser of frequency 𝜔𝑝 (red arrows) and after non-radiative relaxation emits into the cavity mode with light-matter coupling strength 𝑔.
